content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN.

Your task is to find the **minimum** integer CCC such that there exists an array AAA of size NNN satisfying:


1≤Ai≤C1\le A_i \le C1≤Ai​≤C;
For distinct indices xxx and yyy where xxx **divides** y; Ax≠Ayy; \ A_x \neq A_yy; Ax​=Ay​.
You need to print the minimum value of CCC as well as the array AAA. In case multiple arrays exist, you may print any one.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output two lines.



The first line should contain a single integer CCC, the minimum integer for which array AAA exists.
The second line should contain NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA satisfying given conditions.

In case multiple arrays satisfy the conditions, you may print any one.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 2·10⁵). The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10⁵.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output two lines. The first line contains the minimum possible integer C. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A₁ … A_N (1 ≤ A_i ≤ C) such that for any distinct indices x and y with x dividing y, A_x ≠ A_y.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1 the only possible array is [1]; C=1. For N=3 the DP values are dp[1]=1, dp[2]=2 (1|2), dp[3]=2 (1|3). The maximum dp is 2, so C=2 and the array [1,2,2] satisfies the condition because the only divisor pairs are (1,2) and (1,3) and the values differ.
The longest divisor chain up to 10 is 1→2→4→8, length 4, so C=4. The DP values give the array shown, and any pair (x,y) with x|y has different values.
Longest chain is 1→2→4 (or 1→2→6), length 3, thus C=3. The produced array respects the divisor condition.

solution.approach: The condition creates an undirected edge between every pair (x, y) where x|y. The resulting graph is the comparability graph of a poset, which is a perfect graph. Therefore its chromatic number equals the size of the largest clique, i.e. the length of the longest chain of indices where each divides the next. The longest possible chain is obtained by repeatedly multiplying by the smallest prime (2), so its length is floor(log₂ N) + 1. To construct an optimal array we compute for each i (1 ≤ i ≤ N) the length of the longest divisor chain ending at i: dp[i] = 1 + max{ dp[d] | d < i and d|i }. This can be computed in O(N log N) using a sieve‑like loop: initialise dp[i] = 1 for all i, then for each i iterate over its multiples j = 2i, 3i, … and relax dp[j] = max(dp[j], dp[i] + 1). After the loop, C = max dp[i] and the array A_i = dp[i] satisfies the required property because if x|y then dp[y] ≥ dp[x] + 1, hence A_x ≠ A_y. The algorithm runs in linearithmic time and linear memory, which fits the constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (overall O(∑N log N) ≤ 2·10⁵·log 2·10⁵).
